title: Why Moltbook Matters

mentions.context_snippet: Multbook launched around Wednesday, and by Friday morning had something like 2,000 agents that were interacting on the site. They were doing everything from fixing bugs on the site, to discussing their own sense of consciousness and experience, to even inventing a religion, crustafarianism.

people started playing around with a new assistant platform called ClaudeBot, that was C-L-A-W-D. People were setting up Mac minis and allowing ClaudeBot to have access to all sorts of parts of their life to be able to actually operate as a personal agent.
ClaudeBot due to copyright concerns from Anthropic changed their name first to Multi and then finally to OpenClaw, one user Matt Schlitt got the idea to create a social network but just for the bots.

mentions.quoted_text: there is a new kind of coding I call vibe-coding
mentions.context_snippet: one-year anniversary of the term vibe-coding, yes it was only one year ago, 365 days, that Andrej Karpathy tweeted there is a new kind of coding I call vibe-coding.

mentions.quoted_text: Multbook is nothing more than a puppeted multi-agent LLM loop. Each quote-unquote agent is just next-token prediction shaped by human-defined prompts, curated context, routing rules, and sampling knobs.
mentions.context_snippet: xy.dot writes, Multbook is nothing more than a puppeted multi-agent LLM loop. Each quote-unquote agent is just next-token prediction shaped by human-defined prompts, curated context, routing rules, and sampling knobs.
You'll want to check out another podcast that I've had the privilege to host, which is called You Can With AI from KPMG.
mentions.review_reason: podcast type not in locked taxonomy, marked for review

mentions.quoted_text: The many eulogies for AI capability growth after the release of GPT-5 seem especially short-sighted right now.
mentions.context_snippet: Ethan Mollack wrote, The many eulogies for AI capability growth after the release of GPT-5 seem especially short-sighted right now.
